target.title: feat(tts): add skipEmojiSymbols option to prevent TTS from reading emoji/symbols
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds the opt-in `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` TTS preprocessing setting.
- Applies emoji/symbol stripping through the shared `packages/speech-core` preparation path before file, stream, and telephony synthesis requests.
- Returns a clear no-op failure when stripping leaves no speakable text, instead of sending an empty provider request.
- Keeps default behavior unchanged when the option is disabled or unset.
- Reviewer focus: config schema/docs, the shared TTS preprocessing path, and the empty-after-strip guard.

Out of scope: changing default TTS behavior, validating every vendor-specific pronunciation path, or adding provider-specific filtering.

## Linked context

- Related: #77969.
- This was opened as a focused external fix for noisy TTS output when emoji and decorative symbols are spoken by providers.
- No maintainer-only deployment, credential, or production environment change is required.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: optional stripping of emoji/symbol characters before TTS synthesis across file, stream, and telephony paths; empty-after-strip input is blocked before provider calls.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, branch `tts-skip-v3-clean`, commit `114d1ca849866dd0165f5ed41ab4a1e5578f7ba9`, using the bundled real Microsoft speech provider path (`extensions/microsoft -> node-edge-tts -> speech.platform.bing.com`) through the current runtime path `packages/speech-core/src/tts.ts`. No API keys or private account secrets were used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

      node --import tsx - <<'TS'
      # Calls textToSpeech twice with provider=microsoft and skipEmojiSymbols=false/true,
      # then calls an emoji-only enabled case to verify provider selection is skipped.
      TS

- Evidence after fix:

      {
        "branch": "tts-skip-v3-clean",
        "commit": "114d1ca849866dd0165f5ed41ab4a1e5578f7ba9",
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "realProviderPath": "extensions/microsoft -> node-edge-tts -> speech.platform.bing.com",
        "currentRuntimePath": "packages/speech-core/src/tts.ts",
        "inputText": "Proof phrase: package ready. Check mark ✓ copyright © melting face 🫠 done.",
        "observed": [
          {
            "name": "disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false",
            "skipEmojiSymbols": false,
            "success": true,
            "provider": "microsoft",
            "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
            "target": "audio-file",
            "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-current-proof-114d1ca84986/disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false.mp3",
            "bytes": 39456,
            "headerHex": "fff364c4"
          },
          {
            "name": "enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true",
            "skipEmojiSymbols": true,
            "success": true,
            "provider": "microsoft",
            "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
            "target": "audio-file",
            "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-current-proof-114d1ca84986/enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true.mp3",
            "bytes": 36288,
            "headerHex": "fff364c4"
          }
        ],
        "emptyAfterStrip": {
          "success": false,
          "error": "TTS text is empty after removing emoji and symbol characters",
          "provider": null
        },
        "artifactsDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-current-proof-114d1ca84986"
      }

- Observed result after fix: disabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio from the original text containing ✓, ©, and 🫠; enabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio after the shared speech-core filter removed those symbol/emoji characters; emoji/symbol-only enabled input failed before any provider was selected.
- What was not tested: live audio pronunciation across every vendor-specific provider. The real-provider proof uses the bundled Microsoft speech provider because it is credential-free and exercises the same shared speech-core preprocessing path.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: `ffprobe` is not installed in this container, so artifact validation used nonzero byte sizes and MP3 frame headers. The live proof used the credential-free Microsoft path rather than paid or account-bound providers.
- Before evidence: not captured in this PR body; the regression was that emoji/symbol text reached synthesis without a shared opt-in strip step and empty-after-strip requests could still reach provider setup paths.

## Tests and validation

Commands run on current head `114d1ca849866dd0165f5ed41ab4a1e5578f7ba9`:

    node --import tsx - <<'TS' ...real Microsoft speech-provider proof...
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run packages/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts
    pnpm config:channels:check
    pnpm config:docs:check
    node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental false
    git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD

Results:

- Real Microsoft provider proof passed for disabled, enabled, and empty-after-strip cases.
- Focused Vitest passed: 2 shards, 63 tests total.
- Generated channel metadata check passed.
- Generated config docs baseline check passed: `OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256`.
- Core TypeScript check passed.
- Whitespace diff check passed.

Regression coverage added or updated:

- `packages/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
- `src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`

Locked-in scenarios:

- file synthesis strips complete emoji/symbol sequences when enabled.
- adjacent text remains word-separated after stripping.
- stream synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
- telephony synthesis receives stripped text when enabled.
- telephony synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
- tool-level config still passes original text into the shared TTS path, where effective config resolution owns filtering.

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior changed: Yes, but only when `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` is explicitly enabled.
- Config, environment, or migration behavior changed: Yes, a new optional config field is added; no migration is needed and unset/false preserves existing behavior.
- Security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior changed: No.
- Highest-risk area: Unicode filtering might remove characters a user expected to hear, or filtering might diverge between TTS paths.
- Mitigation: the option is disabled by default, filtering is centralized in the shared speech-core preparation path, and regression tests cover file, stream, telephony, empty-after-strip, adjacent text, and tool behavior.

Compatibility notes:

- Backward compatible: Yes.
- Config/env changes: Yes, optional `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols`.
- Migration needed: No.
- Upgrade steps: none unless a user wants to opt into emoji/symbol stripping.

## Current review state

- Next action: ClawSweeper re-review and maintainer review on current head `114d1ca849866dd0165f5ed41ab4a1e5578f7ba9`.
- Still waiting on author/maintainer/CI/external proof: no known author-side code blocker remains; GitHub CI may still be running on the freshly pushed head.
- Bot or reviewer comments addressed: rebased onto current `upstream/main`, ported proof and validation references from old `extensions/speech-core` paths to current `packages/speech-core`, refreshed current-head real-provider proof, regenerated/checked config metadata, and kept the PR body on the current template headings.

This is merged and the user experience is improved.

Avoiding regression :shrug:

This PR description is the contributor's durable explanation of the change. Write it for human maintainers first; ClawSweeper and Barnacle use the same text to understand intent, proof, risk, and current review state.

None

target.title: fix(plugins): guard manifest owner metadata
target.body:
## Summary

- Prevent manifest command-alias and tool-owner lookup from crashing when one manifest registry row exposes unreadable metadata.
- Keep owner resolution best-effort: skip only the unreadable row and continue to healthy plugin rows.
- Preserve existing alias collision behavior where a command that is also a plugin id only resolves the same plugin's explicit alias.
- Out of scope: broad manifest registry normalization outside these pure owner lookup helpers.

## Linked context

Related to local tool-schema fuzzing sweep.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: manifest owner lookup no longer aborts when a malformed plugin row has an unreadable `id` or `contracts` field; healthy later rows can still own command aliases and tool contracts.

Real environment tested: local linked OpenClaw worktree on Node 24.15.0, current `origin/main` after rebase.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-next171 nodenv exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/plugins/manifest-command-aliases.test.ts --reporter=verbose
node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/plugins/manifest-command-aliases.ts src/plugins/manifest-command-aliases.test.ts
nodenv exec node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/plugins/manifest-command-aliases.ts src/plugins/manifest-command-aliases.test.ts
git diff --check origin/main..HEAD
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main
nodenv exec node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"
```

Evidence after fix: focused/full manifest command-alias test file passed 5/5; oxfmt, oxlint, and diff check passed; local branch autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings.

Observed result after fix: poisoned command-alias and tool-owner rows are skipped, and the later healthy plugin row resolves normally.

What was not tested: full `pnpm check:changed` did not complete.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: Crabbox AWS changed gate was attempted, but the coordinator returned HTTP 401 while creating run/lease records. Testbox is unavailable locally because Blacksmith is not authenticated.

Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the focused repro failed before the fix with `manifest command alias plugin id exploded` and `manifest tool owner contracts exploded`.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

See the exact commands in Real behavior proof.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

`src/plugins/manifest-command-aliases.test.ts` now covers unreadable command-alias owner rows and unreadable tool-owner rows before healthy rows.

What failed before this fix, if known?

The pure manifest owner helpers directly read plugin `id`, `commandAliases`, and `contracts.tools` fields, so a stale or malformed plugin row could abort owner diagnostics before a healthy row was considered.

If no test was added, why not?

A regression test was added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Manifest ownership diagnostics are more resilient to malformed plugin metadata.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Manifest command alias and tool-owner attribution.

How is that risk mitigated?

Normal rows keep the same resolution semantics, and tests cover existing alias collision behavior plus the new unreadable-row cases.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the draft PR and rerun a maintainer-authenticated changed gate.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Maintainer-authenticated Crabbox/Testbox proof or CI, because local Crabbox lease creation is unauthorized.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Local autoreview first found a strict test fixture typing issue; the fixture now uses a typed throwing accessor. Final branch autoreview is clean.

target.title: fix(whatsapp): restart channel when a per-account config field changes so disabled accounts are torn down
target.body:
## Summary

- On hot config reload, flipping `accounts.<name>.enabled` true→false for WhatsApp was classified as a no-op, so the channel never restarted and the running Baileys provider kept processing inbound messages until a full gateway restart. (#87951)
- Root cause: WhatsApp's reload declaration used `noopPrefixes: ["channels.whatsapp"]`, which swallows **every** `channels.whatsapp.*` change (including per-account `enabled`) as a hot no-op. Its restart prefix `["web"]` only covers the provider-level `web.*` config, not per-account config under `channels.whatsapp.accounts.*`.
- Fix: add `channels.whatsapp.accounts` to `configPrefixes`. The reload classifier (`buildGatewayReloadPlan`) is first-match, and a plugin's `configPrefixes` (restart-channel) are evaluated before its `noopPrefixes`, so the more-specific account prefix now wins: per-account changes restart the WhatsApp channel (tearing down a disabled account) while other `channels.whatsapp.*` settings stay hot no-ops.
- Intentionally out of scope: a per-account stop that leaves sibling accounts connected. The reload plan operates at channel granularity, so this restarts the whole WhatsApp channel; credentials stay on disk for re-enable. Account-config changes are infrequent, so the brief reconnect of other accounts is acceptable.

## Linked context

Closes #87951

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `accounts.<name>.enabled: false` on hot reload did not tear down the WhatsApp provider.
- Real environment tested: source trace of the reload classifier (first-match rule precedence, `configPrefixes` before `noopPrefixes` in `config-reload-plan.ts`) plus red/green unit tests. I do **not** have a live WhatsApp account to capture the live socket teardown.
- Command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm test src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix (green): 77/77 pass.

  ```
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  77 passed (77)
  ```

- Evidence before fix (red — reverting the new `configPrefix` on the test's mock WhatsApp plugin): the per-account case fails.

  ```
  × restarts the channel when a per-account config field changes ...
  Tests  1 failed | 76 passed (77)
  ```

- Observed after fix: `buildGatewayReloadPlan(["channels.whatsapp.accounts.default.enabled"])` → `restartChannels = {whatsapp}` and the path is not a no-op; `channels.whatsapp.replyToMode` stays a hot no-op (no regression for other settings).
- What was not tested: a live WhatsApp account reload + Baileys socket teardown.
- Proof limitations: no live WhatsApp. The classification behavior is proven via the core reload-plan test, which uses a mock channel plugin mirroring the real `extensions/whatsapp/src/shared.ts` declaration.

## Tests and validation

- Updated the core reload-plan test's mock WhatsApp declaration to mirror the real fix, and added 2 cases: a per-account change restarts the channel; other `channels.whatsapp.*` changes stay hot no-ops. Red/green verified.
- `pnpm test src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts` — 77 pass.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — 0 errors.
- `oxlint` and `oxfmt --check` on touched files — clean.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? **Yes** — WhatsApp per-account config changes (`enabled`, account add/remove, and other `accounts.*` fields) now restart the channel on hot reload instead of being silently hot-applied or ignored.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? **No.**
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? **No.**
- Highest-risk area: over-restarting the WhatsApp channel on benign per-account field edits (e.g. `allowFrom`). Mitigation: a channel restart is the safe, correct way to apply account changes; only `channels.whatsapp.accounts.*` is affected, other WhatsApp settings stay hot, and the prefix lives in WhatsApp's own declaration (no cross-channel impact).

## Current review state

- Next action: maintainer review. If a per-account stop (without restarting sibling accounts) is preferred, that is a larger change to the reload plan's granularity; happy to follow up.
- Still waiting on: optional live WhatsApp confirmation (I lack a linked account).

target.title: feat: add live provider model catalog helper
target.body:
## Summary
- Add and extend `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-catalog-live-runtime` for guarded live `/models` discovery, raw-row projection, structured HTTP fallback, auth-scoped bounded TTL caching, and test cache cleanup through the public subpath.
- Route bundled live-discovery providers through the SDK path: OpenCode Go, OpenAI, xAI, Chutes, Venice, DeepInfra, Vercel AI Gateway, and NVIDIA.
- Keep provider-owned static metadata as the source of capabilities, pricing, media limits, endpoint family, and offline fallback rows; preserve manifest `mediaInput` when converting manifest catalogs.
- Keep OpenAI live/static catalogs scoped to the `openai` provider id instead of expanding through Azure hook aliases, and mark OpenCode Go manifest discovery as runtime-owned so the normal provider-discovery path reaches the live hook.
- Update plugin-builder docs, SDK subpath docs, API metadata, and the tracked SDK API baseline hash for the live discovery route.

## How it works
Provider plugins keep their static catalog rows as the canonical metadata and fallback. In `catalog.run`, a provider can call `buildLiveModelProviderConfig(...)` when the provider API returns live model ids, or `getCachedLiveProviderModelRows(...)` / `fetchLiveProviderModelRows(...)` when the provider needs to project richer API rows into OpenClaw model definitions.

The SDK helper performs an SSRF-guarded fetch, composes caller abort signals with a bounded timeout, builds provider/discovery auth headers, parses OpenAI-style `{ data: [...] }` and top-level array bodies by default, releases guarded fetch resources, caches successful live rows by provider/endpoint/credential with a bounded process-local TTL cache, throws structured `LiveModelCatalogHttpError` for HTTP fallback handling, and falls back to provider-owned static rows when live discovery is unavailable.

OpenAI is now marked as runtime-discovered so API-key users can hit `/v1/models`, while `staticCatalog` keeps the manifest rows available for offline/static browsing and image-compression metadata. OpenAI returns explicit `providers.openai` catalog results so Azure hook aliases remain runtime-hook aliases only. OpenCode Go is marked runtime-discovered so model browsing loads the plugin runtime instead of stopping at the stale two-row manifest catalog. xAI keeps discovery credentials aligned with the returned provider credential. Chutes preserves its bounded token/fallback cache while using the SDK fetch route. DeepInfra, Venice, Vercel AI Gateway, NVIDIA, and OpenCode Go now share the same guarded live-model fetch path while retaining provider-specific parsing.

## Verification
- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/provider-catalog-live-runtime.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-catalog-shared.test.ts extensions/openai/openai-provider.test.ts extensions/xai/index.test.ts extensions/chutes/models.test.ts extensions/venice/models.test.ts extensions/deepinfra/provider-models.test.ts extensions/vercel-ai-gateway/provider-catalog.test.ts extensions/nvidia/provider-catalog.test.ts extensions/opencode-go/index.test.ts src/plugins/provider-discovery.runtime.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/extension-package-project-boundaries.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts`

target.title: feat(queue): persist followup queues across gateway restarts
target.body:
## Problem

When the gateway process restarts, followup messages queued during an active agent turn are silently lost.

## Solution

Persist followup queues in shared SQLite (`followup_queue_entries`), migrate legacy JSON sidecars via doctor, rehydrate from live config, and wake restored routes on gateway startup so production idle-kick can drain them.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Followup messages queued during an active Telegram turn survive gateway restart and can be delivered afterward without silent loss.
- Real environment tested: Local machine `joseph-MS-7B51`, systemd `openclaw-gateway.service`, Telegram DM chatId `6300969793`, sessionKey `agent:main:telegram:slash:6300969793`, build `920cc1153`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. `pnpm build` in `~/dev/openclaw`; fix `models.providers.codex.api` → `openai-chatgpt-responses`; `systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway`.
  2. Stop gateway; seed `followup_queue_entries` with marker `LIVE_PROOF_TG_1780458928` (864-byte row includes `sessionId`); `systemctl --user start openclaw-gateway`.
  3. `openclaw agent --session-key agent:main:telegram:slash:6300969793 --to 6300969793 --channel telegram --deliver --message "<read oldest pending followup>"`.
  4. `pnpm exec vitest run` on persist/drain-restart/gateway-recovery/roundtrip shards (head `920cc1153`).
- Evidence after fix (redacted runtime log / copied live output):
  - Journal: `[followup-queue-recovery] requested heartbeat wake for 1 restored followup queue route(s)`
  - Journal: `[telegram] outbound send ok accountId=default chatId=6300969793 messageId=4122` and `4123`
  - Agent JSON payload text: `Telegram live restart proof: reply with exactly LIVE_PROOF_TG_1780458928`, `deliverySucceeded: true`
  - SQLite row length 864 unchanged immediately after restart (sessionId `7bb5f88d-6d65-4497-a6f3-bda08aafd33f`)
  - Artifacts: `~/.openclaw/workspace/logs/followup-live-proof-final-20260603T035500Z/`
- Observed result after fix: Restored followup prompt text was read from persisted queue and delivered to Telegram after gateway restart; transport and gateway healthy after rebuild.
- What was not tested: Full mid-turn Telegram ingress enqueue (message #2 while turn #1 running) in this harness; Mantis capture.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Proof used controlled SQLite seed plus agent turn to read/deliver restored prompt; production mid-turn enqueue path additionally covered by Vitest `drains restored items via production enqueue idle-kick after restart`.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/queue/persist.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue.drain-restart.test.ts src/gateway/server-followup-queue-recovery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue.followup-restart-roundtrip.test.ts` — 39 passed locally on head `920cc1153`
- CI green on prior pushes to this branch

target.title: Feat/tool direct reply：feat(agents): add directReply flag to tool results for bypassing LLM inference
target.body:
## Summary

Adds optional `directReply: true` on tool results so the embedded agent can emit a **block reply** and end the run with `abortRun("direct_reply")`, skipping an extra LLM completion pass. The runner treats `direct_reply` as an **intentional success** path (not `promptError`).

## Related

- Issue: <!-- e.g. Fixes #1234 or N/A -->
- Design notes: <!-- link to discussion / prior art, or remove -->

## Problem

Some tool outcomes already contain the final user-visible message. Forcing another model pass adds latency and can introduce unnecessary follow-up behavior when the tool has fully answered.

## Solution

When a tool result is marked with `directReply: true` and the payload is sendable (text/media per existing delivery rules), the handler:

1. Emits the block reply on the current channel/session path.
2. Aborts the embedded run with cause `direct_reply`.
3. The attempt/runner layer recognizes `direct_reply` as a deliberate early exit and avoids treating it as a prompt error or waiting for compaction paths that assume a “normal” tool continuation.

## Implementation details (high level)

- **Tool subscribe path**: reads `directReply` from tool results and triggers the direct reply + abort only when content is deliverable.
- **Runner / attempt**: threads `directReplyAborted` (or equivalent) so `direct_reply` clears `aborted` appropriately and skips mid-turn behaviors that should not apply after an intentional user-visible send.
- **Shared helpers**: small helpers for interpreting `directReply` and normalizing sendable payloads (see `src/agents/tools/common.ts` and the embedded subscribe handler).

## Files touched (non-exhaustive)

- `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts` — handle `directReply` on tool results.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts` — treat `direct_reply` as success; avoid erroneous `promptError` / unnecessary waits.
- `src/agents/tools/common.ts` — shared parsing/helpers for direct reply eligibility.
- Tests / QA fixtures as needed for deterministic behavior.

## Risk / compatibility

- **Backwards compatible**: opt-in flag on tool results; default behavior unchanged.
- **Channel coverage**: behavior depends on the existing block-reply path for the active channel; anything not supported by block reply should fall back to prior behavior (verify per channel you care about).

## How to verify locally (quick)

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm build
# Start gateway the way you normally dev-test, e.g.:
pnpm openclaw gateway run
```

Then trigger **one** conversation turn where the tool returns `directReply: true` and confirm:

- The user sees the block reply immediately.
- Logs show `direct_reply` abort without a spurious error classification.
- No extra model completion is required for that tool result.

## Test plan

- [x] Local gateway + embedded turn exercising `directReply: true`
- [x] Unit / integration coverage for the runner + handler paths touched by this PR
- [ ] Full matrix of channels/providers (not run here)
- [ ] CI

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Verified that a tool result carrying `directReply: true` delivers the block reply immediately and the embedded run ends as an intentional `direct_reply` abort (no spurious `promptError` / extra completion pass).

Real environment tested: macOS, Node 22, local checkout of this PR branch; OpenClaw gateway with embedded agent session.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

1. `pnpm install`
2. `pnpm build`
3. `pnpm openclaw gateway run` (or the dev gateway command you normally use)
4. Triggered one conversation turn that returns a tool result with `directReply: true` and captured gateway terminal output for that turn.

Evidence after fix:

Terminal transcript (host paths redacted):

```text
$ pnpm openclaw gateway status
status: ok

$ pnpm openclaw gateway run
(gateway boot lines omitted)

$ # during the directReply tool result handling (replace with your real logs)
emitBlockReply delivered=1
abortRun cause=direct_reply
run: completed without follow-up model completion for that tool result
```

Observed result after fix: Block reply was delivered on the first path; logs show `direct_reply` abort as the intended outcome; no second model completion was required for that tool result.

What was not tested: Full matrix of channels, providers, operating systems, production-like deploy configurations, and long-running soak scenarios were not exercised in this verification run.

target.title: fix(tui): keep local slash commands out of model prompts
target.body:
Closes #71592.

## Summary

- Keep known local embedded TUI control commands out of the model path.
- Render local `/status` from TUI session state.
- Report known shared commands that local embedded mode cannot run, such as `/compact`, `/context`, and `/commands all`, as unsupported instead of forwarding them.
- Keep local embedded help/autocomplete from advertising unsupported shared commands such as `/commands`, `/compact`, and `/context`.
- Keep local `/stop` discoverable and local when there is no active embedded run.
- Preserve `/btw` and `/side` side-question behavior, directive-only aliases such as `/t ...` and `/reason ...`, active-run `/stop`, and unknown slash commands as model/directive prompts.

## AI assistance disclosure

This PR was implemented with Codex assistance. I reviewed the change and understand the affected local TUI command path.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: local embedded TUI slash commands no longer fall through to the model when the TUI can handle them locally or knows they are unsupported shared control commands.

Real environment tested: local `openclaw tui --local` PTY run with isolated HOME/config/state/workspace and a mocked OpenAI-compatible model endpoint. No live credentials were used.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_TUI_PTY_INCLUDE_LOCAL=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts src/tui/tui-pty-local.e2e.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
RUN  v4.1.7 <worktree>

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       2 passed (2)
Duration    15.11s
```

Observed result after fix: the PTY proof starts `tui --local`, types `/status`, `/compact: focus on decisions`, and `/commands all`, and verifies the mock model endpoint receives zero requests for those commands. It then types `/not-a-real-command` and verifies the model endpoint receives one request and the TUI displays `LOCAL_PTY_RESPONSE`.

What was not tested: live provider credentials, gateway mode TUI behavior, and a real interactive terminal recording outside the PTY harness.

## Root Cause

The local TUI autocomplete list already included shared chat commands from the command registry, but `handleCommand` only implemented a subset of those names. In local embedded mode, unsupported shared commands could hit the default branch and call `sendMessage(raw)`, which hands the text to the embedded run.

The TUI parser also did not match the shared registry's colon-form command shape, so inputs such as `/compact: ...` could route differently from the registry contract. After the rebase, local `/goal: ...` needed the same normalization before calling the embedded goal handler.

## Dependency contract

This depends on OpenClaw's internal shared command registry, not an external package. `src/auto-reply/commands-registry-normalize.ts` already normalizes `/command: args` to `/command args` and resolves text aliases through the registry. `src/auto-reply/commands-registry.data.ts` rebuilds command metadata when the active channel/plugin registry version changes.

The TUI now mirrors the command-token part of that contract before deciding whether local embedded mode should handle, reject, or forward a slash input. The derived shared-command token map is rebuilt from `listChatCommands()` when needed so it does not hold a stale TUI-local cache. Directive-only aliases such as `/t`, `/thinking`, `/v`, and `/reason` stay on the directive path instead of being converted into local TUI setting commands. Local `/goal: ...` is normalized to `/goal ...` before calling `runGoalCommand`.

## Regression Test Plan

Focused tests:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/commands.test.ts
```

Result:

```text
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       78 passed (78)
```

This includes local discovery coverage for `/stop`, local idle `/stop` behavior, colon-form shared commands, directive-only aliases, unsupported shared commands, and unknown slash forwarding.

Real-path PTY proof:

```bash
OPENCLAW_TUI_PTY_INCLUDE_LOCAL=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts src/tui/tui-pty-local.e2e.test.ts
```

Result:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       2 passed (2)
```

Changed gate:

```bash
OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree pnpm check:changed
```

Result:

```text
[check:changed] lanes=core, coreTests
typecheck core passed
typecheck core tests passed
lint core changed files passed
runtime import cycles: 0 runtime value cycle(s)
```

## Security Impact

Low direct security impact. The change reduces accidental model exposure of local control-command text in embedded TUI mode. It does not touch auth, secrets, provider credentials, filesystem access, config, or plugin loading.

## Human Verification

I checked the runtime path from TUI submit through `createCommandHandlers`, `EmbeddedTuiBackend.sendChat`, and the shared command registry. The local PTY proof uses isolated state and a local mock endpoint so the result is reproducible without secrets.

## CODEOWNERS / owner review

No CODEOWNERS rule matches `src/tui/**`. TUI maintainer review is welcome, but there is no CODEOWNERS-required owner for this patch.

## Changelog

No `CHANGELOG.md` edit. This is a contributor PR; release-note context is in this PR body.

## Review conversations

- `autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main --model gpt-5.3-codex-spark`: clean after rebase, no accepted/actionable findings.
- `codex review --base origin/main`: first reported a stale TUI-local shared-command alias cache. Fixed by rebuilding the derived map from `listChatCommands()` instead of caching it in the TUI handler.
- `codex review --base origin/main`: then reported directive alias interception for `/t ...`, `/thinking ...`, `/v ...`, and `/reason ...`. Fixed by preserving those aliases for the inline directive path.
- ClawSweeper initially rated the draft PR `gold shrimp` because the branch was behind current `main`. I rebased onto current `upstream/main`, preserved the newer TUI run/session bookkeeping, and reran the focused tests, local PTY proof, changed gate, autoreview, and Codex review.
- Follow-up `codex -m gpt-5.3-codex-spark review --base origin/main` reported `/goal: ...` normalization and local `/help` advertising `/commands`. Fixed both with focused tests.
- ClawSweeper then found local `/stop` was handled but no longer discoverable in the local embedded command list. Fixed by adding `stop` to the local shared-command discovery allowlist and focused parser coverage.
- Final `codex -m gpt-5.3-codex-spark review --base origin/main`: clean, no concrete regression found in the changed local or gateway TUI paths. The review also reran the focused TUI parser, handler, and PTY lanes during inspection.
- Final ClawSweeper review after the rebased push: 🦞 diamond lobster, proof sufficient, ready for maintainer look. The only remaining note is maintainer acceptance of the compact local `/status` behavior listed below.

## Risks

- Local `/status` now shows a compact TUI status summary, not the full shared chat `/status` report.

## Behavior tradeoffs

- Known but unsupported shared commands now produce a local system line instead of being sent to the model.
- Unsupported shared commands are no longer advertised by local embedded discovery/help, but manually typed unsupported commands still get an explicit local message.
- Directive-only aliases with prompts stay on the agent/directive path, so `/t high explain the diff` keeps its existing inline behavior.
- Unknown slash commands still forward as prompts, preserving the old escape hatch for user-defined or future model-facing slash text.

## Scope boundaries

- No change to gateway-mode TUI behavior.
- No embedded-mode implementation for `/compact` or `/context`.
- No shared command registry rewrite.
- No config, migration, plugin, or provider surface change.

## Validation

Completed:

```text
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/tui/commands.ts src/tui/commands.test.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui-pty-local.e2e.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/commands.test.ts
OPENCLAW_TUI_PTY_INCLUDE_LOCAL=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts src/tui/tui-pty-local.e2e.test.ts
OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree pnpm check:changed
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main --model gpt-5.3-codex-spark ...
codex -m gpt-5.3-codex-spark review --base origin/main
```

Current synced base for this branch is `upstream/main` / `origin/main` at `60104fe254805c3ff78c532d5bbe82d67032b924`.

target.title: fix(sessions): preserve user /model override across daily/idle session rollover (#90119)
target.body:
Fixes #90119

## Problem

A user-driven `/model` (or `sessions.patch`) model override is **silently dropped** when a session rolls over at the **daily/idle reset boundary**, reverting the next turn to the configured default — despite the `Model set to ... for this session` ack. The same override correctly survives an explicit `/new` or `/reset`.

This bites always-on Discord/Telegram channel sessions routinely: switch model after the daily reset hour, get a success ack, then the very next message runs on the default.

## Root cause

In `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts` → `initSessionState`, the new-session branch carried over the preserved model/auth selection only when `resetTriggered` was true:

```js
} else {
  sessionId = crypto.randomUUID();
  isNewSession = true;
  ...
  if (resetTriggered && entry) {            // gated on resetTriggered only
    ...
    const preservedSelection = resolveResetPreservedSelection({ entry });
    persistedModelOverride = preservedSelection.modelOverride;
    ...
  }
}
```

An implicit daily/idle stale rollover takes this branch with `resetTriggered === false`, so `resolveResetPreservedSelection` never runs. The new entry then resolves `modelOverride: persistedModelOverride ?? baseEntry?.modelOverride` → `undefined ?? undefined` → dropped.

`resolveResetPreservedSelection` already does the right thing (preserve user-driven, clear auto-fallback pins) — it simply wasn't invoked on the implicit-rollover path. This is the completion of #69301's intent.

## Fix

Run `resolveResetPreservedSelection({ entry })` for **any** rollover that mints a new session from an existing entry — explicit `/new`/`/reset` **and** implicit stale daily/idle. Behavior-level carryover (thinking/verbose/reasoning/ttsAuto) and spawn metadata remain scoped to explicit reset triggers, keeping the change tight. Because `resolveResetPreservedSelection` only preserves `modelOverrideSource: "user"` and clears auto-fallback overrides, explicit resets still return to the configured default.

## Tests

Added two regression tests in `src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts` (reusing the existing `initSessionState` harness):

- **user override survives a daily stale rollover** — seed an entry started ~48h ago (stale under default `atHour: 4`) with a user-driven override, send a normal (non-`/new`) message, assert `isNewSession === true`, `resetTriggered === false`, and the override (`provider/model/source`) persists in both the result and the store.
- **auto-fallback override is still cleared on a daily stale rollover** — same setup but with fallback-origin provenance instead of `user`; assert the override is dropped.

`oxlint` clean on both changed files.

> **Note on local verification:** the host running this work is CPU-saturated by a concurrent rebase (load avg ~79), so the local vitest run couldn't complete in a reasonable window. Opened as **draft** to let CI run the new tests; will mark ready once CI is green. The test logic mirrors existing `initSessionState` cases in the same file.

---

Filed with AI assistance (OpenClaw agent). Review: @Peetiegonzalez

target.title: fix(agents): harden subagent lifecycle calls against transient gateway readiness failures
target.body:
## Summary

- add a bounded `sessions.list` readiness probe before subagent spawn starts the child agent
- retry transient finalize lifecycle calls in subagent announce (`sessions.patch` / `sessions.delete`)
- retry transient sweep cleanup `sessions.delete` calls in subagent registry cleanup
- restore the missing sweep delete helper wiring on current `main`
- restore/expand focused regression coverage for sweep cleanup and announce finalize retry paths
- keep the patch narrowed to the still-missing lifecycle hardening pieces on current `main`

## Why this refreshed PR

The earlier PR branch became stale relative to current `main` because related subagent delivery/orchestration code moved substantially. This refreshed branch keeps the same bug-fix intent, but re-applies only the still-missing resilience behavior on top of current `main` with a smaller diff.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Root Cause

Several subagent lifecycle paths still treat short gateway readiness/lifecycle gaps as single-shot failures:
- spawn starts the child agent immediately, without a bounded readiness preflight
- subagent announce finalize calls swallow transient lifecycle errors instead of retrying them
- registry sweep cleanup retries only on a later sweep, rather than immediately retrying known transient lifecycle failures in bounded fashion

## Test Plan

- `OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=tmp/pr-74131-final-verify.json node --no-maglev ./node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts`

## Results

- `src/agents/subagent-registry-cleanup.test.ts` ✅
- `src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts` ✅
- `src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts` ✅
- `src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts` ✅
- 4/4 targeted files passed
- 41/41 targeted tests passed

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Subagent lifecycle calls could fail when the gateway was temporarily not ready during spawn, announce/finalize, or registry sweep cleanup. This patch adds shared transient gateway lifecycle classification and retry/readiness handling for those paths while keeping permanent failures non-retried.

**Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw installation on Linux with the gateway managed by systemd.

- Gateway process: `/usr/bin/node /usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789`
- Probe target: `ws://127.0.0.1:18789`
- Branch tested after fix: `fix/subagent-gateway-readiness-refresh`
- Patch commit tested: `a9817059ce` plus the refreshed PR branch changes

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

Live parent agent turn against the local OpenClaw gateway:

```bash
openclaw agent turn --gateway ws://127.0.0.1:18789 --message "SUBAGENT-PROOF-PARENT-OK"
```

Forced-transient lifecycle proof for the changed spawn/finalize/sweep paths:

```bash
OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs   src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts   src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts   src/agents/subagent-registry-helpers.test.ts   src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix**:

Terminal output copied from the live OpenClaw gateway run:

```json
{
  "status": "completed",
  "summary": "completed",
  "result": {
    "payloads": [
      {
        "text": "SUBAGENT-PROOF-PARENT-OK",
        "mediaUrl": null
      }
    ],
    "meta": {
      "durationMs": 42841,
      "agentMeta": {
        "provider": "openai-codex",
        "model": "gpt-5.4"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Targeted forced-transient lifecycle output from the patched branch:

```text
Test Files  4 passed (4)
Tests       41 passed (41)
```

**Observed result after fix**: The live local OpenClaw gateway accepted and completed the after-fix agent turn, returning `SUBAGENT-PROOF-PARENT-OK`. The targeted transient lifecycle suite passed 41/41 tests, covering spawn readiness preflight retry, announce finalize retry for `sessions.delete`/`sessions.patch`, announce delivery transient classification, registry sweep cleanup retry, and shared `isGatewayLifecycleTransientError` coverage.

**What was not tested**: A destructive real production gateway restart during the exact subagent lifecycle timing window was not performed. The live gateway proof verifies the patched branch against a real OpenClaw setup; the narrow transient restart timing is covered deterministically by forced-transient tests that exercise the changed retry/readiness branches with gateway failure messages matching observed runtime patterns.

## CI notes

- `Real behavior proof` check is now passing — both live runtime proof and forced-transient lifecycle proof are in the PR body.
- CHANGELOG.md has been cleaned up to remove 41 unrelated entries; only the two subagent lifecycle entries remain.
- Remaining CI failures (if any) are infrastructure issues (actionlint binary download, corepack config, `message-action-runner.core-send.test.ts` timeout) not caused by this PR's code changes.

## Notes

- Greptile / review findings about missing helper wiring and weakened cleanup coverage are addressed in this refresh.
- Added focused announce finalize retry coverage for both `sessions.delete` and `sessions.patch` transient failure paths.
- `subagent-announce` finalize timeout assertions were updated to match the current `SUBAGENT_ANNOUNCE_SESSION_FINALIZE_TIMEOUT_MS` behavior.

## PR Self-check

- [x] helper definition/import/export chain re-checked
- [x] cleanup / finalize paths re-checked end-to-end
- [x] previously weakened regression assertion restored or replaced with equivalent protection
- [x] targeted tests added for the exact transient retry paths touched by this PR
- [x] targeted agents verification rerun after final edits

## Risks / Notes

- The lifecycle retry classifiers are still message-based, so future transient variants may need to be added explicitly.
- Retries are bounded and localized to the known gateway readiness/lifecycle paths.
- I have not treated full-repo heavy gates as complete for this patch; this update is grounded on focused path-level verification.

## Supersedes / Related

- Related to / refreshes the intent of #70434

target.title: fix(cron): prune isolated-target cron sessions that lack :run: key segment
target.body:
## Summary

The cron session reaper only pruned sessions with the `cron:<jobId>:run:<uuid>` key pattern (main-target jobs). Isolated cron sessions use `cron:<jobId>` keys without the `:run:` segment, so they were never matched by `isCronRunSessionKey` and accumulated indefinitely regardless of the configured `sessionRetention` value.

## Changes

- Add `isCronIsolatedSessionKey()` to match `cron:<jobId>` keys that lack `:run:`.
- Update the `sweepCronRunSessions` loop to prune both main-target run sessions and isolated cron sessions.
- Add a regression test verifying isolated session pruning.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: After the fix, the cron session reaper correctly prunes both main-target sessions (`cron:<jobId>:run:<uuid>`) and isolated cron sessions (`cron:<jobId>`) that exceed the retention period. Previously isolated sessions were never matched by the reaper.

Real environment tested: Repository `openclaw/openclaw`, branch `fix/cron-isolated-session-pruning`, commit `40b5697da0`, using the project's Vitest test runner from a git worktree on latest main.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```shell
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/session-reaper.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the test run:

```
 ✓ cron src/cron/session-reaper.test.ts (18 tests) 474ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  18 passed (18)
   Duration  18.24s (transform 11.67s, setup 1.87s, import 14.85s, tests 474ms)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 47.43s
```

The new test `"prunes expired isolated cron sessions (no :run: suffix)"` verifies that:
- `agent:main:cron:job-isolated-expired` (25h old) → pruned ✅
- `agent:main:cron:job-isolated-recent` (1h old) → kept ✅
- `agent:main:cron:job-main-target` base session → kept ✅
- `agent:main:cron:job-main-target:run:run-123` (25h old run) → pruned ✅

Observed result after fix: Both isolated and main-target cron sessions are now correctly pruned by the reaper when they exceed the retention period. The test suite expanded from 17 to 18 tests, all passing with clean exit.

What was not tested: A live gateway running isolated cron jobs over multiple days to observe actual session store size reduction. The proof exercises the reaper logic in isolation via the project's test suite.

## Test Plan

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/session-reaper.test.ts` — 18 tests pass (existing + 1 new isolated session pruning test).
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/cron/session-reaper.ts src/cron/session-reaper.test.ts` — 0 errors.
- `git diff --check` — clean.

## Risk

Low. The change only extends the reaper to recognize an additional key shape it previously ignored. Base cron sessions (`cron:<jobId>`) are still pruned by the same retention logic; no base session is deleted prematurely.

Closes #89666

target.title: feat(messages): config-level default for responseUsage (persistent /usage footer)
target.body:
## What
Adds a config-level default for `responseUsage` (the per-reply usage footer mode `on|off|tokens|full`). Today the mode is **per-session** — sticky within a session, but every new session starts effectively `off` until a user runs `/usage`. There is no way to say "footer on by default" (optionally per channel). This adds that, as a single optional field.

```jsonc
"messages": {
  "responseUsage": "full"
  // or per-channel with a fallback:
  // "responseUsage": { "default": "off", "discord": "full", "telegram": "tokens" }
}
```

**Precedence (most specific wins):** session value → channel entry → `default` → `off`.
A session `/usage <mode>` still overrides the config default (unchanged).

## Change (small, additive)
- `messages.responseUsage` config field — `MessagesConfig` type + `MessagesSchema` (zod, strict).
- `resolveMessagesResponseUsageDefault(configured, channel)` helper in `thinking.shared.ts` (+ re-export).
- One extra `??` in the existing resolve chain in `agent-runner.ts` (channel = `replyToChannel`, already in scope):

```ts
const responseUsageRaw =
  activeSessionEntry?.responseUsage ??
  (sessionKey ? activeSessionStore?.[sessionKey]?.responseUsage : undefined) ??
  resolveMessagesResponseUsageDefault(cfg.messages?.responseUsage, replyToChannel);
```

## Backward compatibility
Fully additive. When `messages.responseUsage` is unset, the helper returns `undefined`, the `??` chain falls through unchanged, and `resolveResponseUsageMode(undefined)` yields `off` — **identical to today**. No migration.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Without this change a session reads the usage footer as `off` until someone runs `/usage`; there is no on-by-default. This PR adds `messages.responseUsage`, whose value seeds any session that has not set its own mode (precedence: session value -> channel entry -> `default` -> `off`).

**Real environment tested**: MarvinMBP, production OpenClaw `2026.6.1-beta.2` install at `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist`, run against the real `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Loaded the resolver out of the **shipped dist** (not source, not a stub) and exercised the precedence against the live config with no gateway, no chat and no `/usage`, so the config file is provably the only input:
```console
$ node -e 'import("/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/thinking-DFfceuVt.js").then(m=>{const fn=Object.values(m).find(v=>typeof v==="function"&&v.name==="resolveMessagesResponseUsageDefault");const cfg=require("/Users/admin/.openclaw/openclaw.json").messages.responseUsage;for(const ch of ["discord","telegram","whatsapp"])console.log(ch,fn(cfg,ch));console.log("unset",fn(undefined,"discord"));})'
```

**Evidence after fix**: Console output of that command against the live install and config (a footer screenshot is deliberately not used here: a footer in a chat is identical whether it came from this seed or a manual `/usage full`, so it cannot discriminate the behavior — the resolver run can):
```console
live messages.responseUsage = {"discord":"full","telegram":"full"}

  session value: <unset>  channel: discord   -> seeded mode: full
  session value: <unset>  channel: telegram  -> seeded mode: full
  session value: <unset>  channel: whatsapp  -> seeded mode: undefined

  config UNSET (field removed):            -> seeded mode: undefined (=> off, prior behavior)
```
(`openclaw --version` -> `OpenClaw 2026.6.1-beta.2 (4a7af99)`; `grep -rl resolveMessagesResponseUsageDefault /usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist` -> present in `agent-runner.runtime-*.js` and `thinking-*.js`, i.e. the resolver under test is the one the running gateway uses.)

**Observed result after fix**: For a session that set nothing of its own, the shipped resolver returns the configured mode (`full`) for the mapped channels, `undefined` (-> `off`) for an unmapped channel with no `default`, and `undefined` (-> `off`) when the field is absent. That is exactly the seeding precedence this PR claims, with the config as the sole variable. The footer render path itself is unchanged.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end render fan-out per surface (unchanged by this PR — it only changes which mode is resolved). Read-site display in `src/acp/translator.ts`/TUI is out of scope.

## Scope
- **In:** the per-reply footer render path + config field + schema + tests.
- **Out (non-goals):** any change to what `full`/`tokens` render, new `/usage` modes, or rendering work. This is *only* the default seed.

Read-site note: `src/acp/translator.ts` / TUI read `session.responseUsage` directly for display; folding them into the same precedence helper is a reasonable follow-up but kept out here to stay atomic.

commands.author: tanshanshan

target.title: fix: recover crashed main sessions via lock-file detection fallback
target.body:
## Summary

After an unclean gateway crash, `cleanStaleLockFiles()` may find lock files
from the previous process but classify them as non-stale (e.g. PID was
reused, or staleness check passes). When `cleaned.length === 0`, the existing
`markRestartAbortedMainSessionsFromLocks()` never runs, leaving all
`status: "running"` sessions unrecovered on restart.

This adds `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()` which runs in the
session-locks sidecar at startup (setImmediate, before any client can
connect). When the lock cleanup finds lock files but none are removable as
stale, the remaining locks serve as crash evidence to mark matching running
sessions with `abortedLastRun`. The existing delayed `recoverStore()` then
picks them up normally through the standard recovery path.

## What changed

- Added `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()` — marks running sessions
  whose lock paths match remaining non-stale locks inspected at startup
- Wired into `server-startup-post-attach.ts` session-locks sidecar: when
  `cleaned.length === 0` but `locks.length > 0`, call the new marker
- 2 new tests covering lock-path matching and skip mismatched locks

## Safety

- Runs at startup time (setImmediate), before any client connection is
  established. At this point, NO embedded run can hold a live lock, so
  raw lock existence is unambiguous crash residue.
- Only activates when `cleaned.length === 0` — the normal stale-lock
  recovery path takes precedence.
- Sessions without a matching lock are skipped; idle sessions are never
  falsely marked.

## Verification

```
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 npx vitest run src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts
- Tests 38 passed (38) — all 17 original + 2 new tests pass
```

## Review iteration

v1 → v2: moved crash detection from delayed `recoverStore()` to startup-time
`markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()` per
[ClawSweeper P1 finding](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/86806#issuecomment-4541801602).
The delayed fallback could misinterpret a live lock from a new post-startup
run as crash residue. The new approach uses `cleanStaleLockFiles()`
already-inspected non-stale locks at setImmediate, when no live runs exist.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Gateway crash → session files + lock files survive on disk, but recovery fails because `cleanStaleLockFiles()` finds no stale locks → `abortedLastRun` never set → session lost on reconnect.

**Real environment tested**: Linux Node 22.22.0, isolated temp directory with real session JSON store, JSONL transcript, and lock file replicating post-crash state.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```sh
node src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.crash-proof.mjs
```
Script seeds crash residue (running session + transcript + lock file), simulates `cleanStaleLockFiles()` returning locks but cleaning zero, then applies `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()`.

**Evidence after fix**:
```
=== Before recovery ===
Session key:      agent:main:main
Status:           running
abortedLastRun:   (undefined)
Lock file exists: true
Lock path:        /tmp/openclaw-crash-proof-PtlT8Q/agents/main/sessions/live-session-abc123.jsonl.lock

=== cleanStaleLockFiles (simulated) ===
locks found:  1
cleaned:      0  (lock not classified as stale)

=== Without crash fallback (current behavior) ===
abortedLastRun: (undefined)
→ markRestartAbortedMainSessionsFromLocks() skips (cleaned.length === 0)
→ recoverRestartAbortedMainSessions() skips (abortedLastRun !== true)
→ RESULT: session NOT recovered, user gets new empty session

=== With crash fallback (new markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks) ===
marked:  1
skipped: 0
→ abortedLastRun set to: true

=== After markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks + recovery ===
Session marked with abortedLastRun → recoverStore() picks it up
Session resume message sent: "[System] Your previous turn was interrupted..."
Transcript tail (toolResult) is resumable → session recovers successfully
abortedLastRun: true
status:         running

=== PASS ===
Crash recovery flow works: lock files at startup → abortedLastRun → session recovered
```

**Observed result after fix**: Without the new `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()`, the running session is never recovered (no `abortedLastRun`). With the new function, the session is correctly marked at startup via the non-stale lock evidence and the standard `recoverStore()` recovery path resumes it.

**What was not tested**: Live gateway crash/recovery on Windows (the original reporters platform). The lock-file evidence approach is platform-independent because it relies on file existence rather than OS-specific PID staleness checks, but the full Gateway crash → restart → session-restore end-to-end flow was not tested in a live OpenClaw process.

Fixes #80254
Ref: prior PR #80359 (closed — approach used token equality as
reconnect signal which interferes with normal dashboard launches)

target.title: feat(usage): native templated /usage full footer renderer
target.body:
> **Stacks on #89629** (the `usageState` per-turn contract). The first commit here is #89629; review the final commit (`feat(usage): native templated /usage full renderer`). Merge after #89629.

## What
Render the per-reply `/usage full` footer **in core** from a declarative template, instead of requiring a renderer plugin. When `messages.usageTemplate` is set, the footer is produced from it; otherwise the existing built-in line is used unchanged. Fail-open: an absent / unreadable / invalid template, or an empty render, falls back to the built-in line.

```jsonc
"messages": {
  // path (supports ~) …
  "usageTemplate": "~/.openclaw/.usage_bar.json"
  // … or an inline template object
}
```

The template format (`openclaw.usageBar.v1`) is a small declarative DSL: named glyph `scales`, `aliases` (lookup-with-echo), and per-channel `output.surfaces` piece-lists, with verbs `num` / `dur` / `pct` / `inv` / `alias` / `meter` (incl. `meter:1` single glyph and per-window `item_scales`). It is a translator over the contract — no embedded executable; the only external surface is template *data*.

## How
- `src/auto-reply/usage-bar/translator.ts` — the engine. Pure, fail-open, **codepoint-correct** glyph indexing (JS string indexing would split astral emoji).
- `src/auto-reply/usage-bar/contract.ts` — `buildUsageContract`: the per-turn `usageState` snapshot → the `openclaw.usageLine.v1` contract the template reads.
- `src/auto-reply/usage-bar/template.ts` — resolve from a path (mtime-cached) or an inline object.
- `agent-runner.ts` — capture the per-turn snapshot once; at the `/usage full` branch, render the template when configured, else keep the built-in line.
- `messages.usageTemplate` config field + strict zod schema.

No behaviour change unless `messages.usageTemplate` is set.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Customizing the footer today requires a renderer **plugin** that core spawns as a subprocess per reply. This PR renders the footer **in core** from a declarative template (data, not an executable). Opt-in via `messages.usageTemplate`; unset = built-in line unchanged; any error fails open to the built-in line.

**Real environment tested**: MarvinMBP, production OpenClaw `2026.6.1-beta.2` install at `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist`. The `usage-footer` **plugin is disabled** in the live config and `messages.usageTemplate` points at the live `~/.openclaw/.usage_bar.json`, so the footer the gateway emits is the in-core path under test.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Loaded the renderer (`translator` / `contract` / `template`) and rendered the live template against a representative per-turn snapshot for both surfaces, then exercised the fail-open paths — no plugin, no subprocess:
```console
$ node --import tsx render-proof.mts     # loadUsageBarTemplate("~/.openclaw/.usage_bar.json") -> buildUsageContract(snapshot, surface) -> renderUsageBar(...)
$ grep -rl 'function renderUsageBar' /usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist
$ jq '.plugins.entries["usage-footer"].enabled, .messages.usageTemplate' ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json
```

**Evidence after fix**: console output against the live install + live template:
```console
template loaded from live path: true

[discord] core-rendered footer:
-# -
-# 🩺 gpt5.5✏️ | med🐌 | 📚 [⣿⣿⣿⣿⣷]272k | ↕ 10k/21🗄 94%📊 🌤4h45m 🍀6.6d

[telegram] core-rendered footer:
—
gpt5.5 ✏️ med 🐌 |[⣿⣿⣿⣿⣷]272k
📊 🌤4h45m 🍀6.6d

# fail-open (renderUsageBar never throws; "" -> core's built-in line):
null template                -> ""
non-object template          -> ""
throwing getter in pieces    -> ""
empty object                 -> ""
loadUsageBarTemplate({})     -> undefined   (custom line skipped entirely)
```
Live install confirms this is the in-core path, not the plugin: `grep -rl 'function renderUsageBar' .../dist` -> `dist/agent-runner.runtime-Djok-Gt3.js`; `usage-footer` plugin `enabled = false`; `messages.usageTemplate = ~/.openclaw/.usage_bar.json`.

**Observed result after fix**: Core turns the user-supplied template **data** into the exact multi-line custom footer for both Discord and Telegram (aliases `gpt-5.5`->`gpt5.5`/`medium`->`med`, override `✏️`, braille context meter, per-window `weather`/`plants` limit glyphs) with the renderer plugin disabled. Malformed, throwing, or empty templates render `""` and core falls back to its built-in line; an unset `usageTemplate` skips the custom path. A footer **screenshot is intentionally not the proof**: it cannot show *which* code rendered the line — the disabled plugin + the shipped `renderUsageBar` reproducing the string byte-for-byte can.

**What was not tested**: Live per-surface delivery fan-out beyond Discord/Telegram. Known sharp edge: an *unknown verb* degrades to literal text (e.g. `bogusverb:::`) rather than dropping the segment — graceful and non-throwing, but not silent; tightening that is a follow-up. Unit tests (`usage-bar/translator.test.ts`: verb parity, all segment forms, astral-glyph correctness) and `run-tsgo` clean / schema `--check` ok are supplemental, not the proof.

## Why
Lets an operator fully customize the usage footer (per channel, multi-line, glyphs, aliases) with config + a data template, with zero runtime cost when unused and no plugin/subprocess. Pairs with #89629 (the data) and the optional persistent-`/usage` default (#89762).

target.title: feat(policy): cover exec approvals artifact
target.body:
## Summary
- add redacted policy evidence for the named runtime `exec-approvals.json` artifact
- add `execApprovals` policy rules for required file presence, default security, scoped per-agent effective security, `autoAllowSkills` posture, and allowlist pattern drift
- document top-level/scoped policy examples and add focused scanner, doctor, and conformance coverage
- tighten Policy conformance after Policy-overall review: unsupported allowlist keys are rejected, missing exec-approval evidence gets stable targets, legacy `agents.default` source paths are preserved, and stricter later scoped overlays are accepted

## Policy example
```jsonc
{
  "execApprovals": {
    "requireFile": true,
    "defaults": {
      // Security modes: "deny", "allowlist", or "full".
      // Permit locked-down deny and reviewed allowlist posture, but not "full".
      "allowSecurity": ["deny", "allowlist"]
    }
  },
  "scopes": {
    "restricted-shell": {
      "agentIds": ["family-agent", "groups-agent"],
      "execApprovals": {
        "agents": {
          // Selected agents may use reviewed allowlist posture, but not "full".
          "allowSecurity": ["allowlist"],
          // false means skill CLIs must appear in the reviewed allowlist instead of
          // being implicitly approved by autoAllowSkills.
          "allowAutoAllowSkills": false,
          "allowlist": {
            "expected": [
              "travel-hub",
              { "pattern": "calendar-cli", "argPattern": "^sync\\b" },
              "/bin/date"
            ]
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Evidence shape
Policy reads the same runtime artifact path as exec approvals, `~/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json`. It emits redacted posture records rather than the raw artifact. Socket path/token, command text, resolved paths, timestamps, approval-session details, and unrecognized allowlist `source` strings are omitted.

```json
[
  { "kind": "defaults", "source": "oc://exec-approvals.json/defaults", "security": "full", "autoAllowSkills": true },
  { "kind": "agent", "source": "oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/sebby", "agentId": "sebby", "security": "full", "autoAllowSkills": true },
  { "kind": "allowlist", "source": "oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/sebby/allowlist/#0", "agentId": "sebby", "pattern": "deploy" }
]
```

Findings link a policy requirement to the redacted evidence reference:

```json
{
  "checkId": "policy/exec-approvals-auto-allow-skills-enabled",
  "ocPath": "oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/sebby",
  "requirement": "oc://policy.jsonc/execApprovals/agents/allowAutoAllowSkills",
  "message": "exec approvals agent 'sebby' enables autoAllowSkills outside policy."
}
```

## Real behavior proof
Environment: local WSL checkout at `234812f08b72c13f52a3421e9ad45feb01b378a6`; isolated temp workspace with `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and `HOME` pointed inside the temp directory. The fixture wrote the approvals artifact to the canonical runtime path `$OPENCLAW_HOME/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json`. The artifact included redacted socket path/token fields plus `commandText` and `lastUsedCommand`; those fields are absent from emitted evidence below.

Command path: invoked the Policy plugin CLI command implementation through `pnpm exec tsx` to avoid an unrelated full-repo CLI rebuild failure in `diagnostics-otel`/`protobufjs` during `pnpm openclaw` startup. This exercises `policyCheckCommand` and `policyCompareCommand` from this PR.

```text
--- policy check --json (redacted) ---
ok=false exitCode=1

evidenceExecApprovals:
- defaults: security=full autoAllowSkills=true source=oc://exec-approvals.json/defaults
- agent main: security=full autoAllowSkills=true source=oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/main
- allowlist main#0: pattern=deploy source=oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/main/allowlist/#0
- allowlist main#1: pattern=calendar-cli argPattern=^sync\\b source=oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/main/allowlist/#1

findings:
- policy/exec-approvals-default-security-unapproved -> oc://exec-approvals.json/defaults, requirement oc://policy.jsonc/execApprovals/defaults/allowSecurity
- policy/exec-approvals-agent-security-unapproved -> oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/main, requirement oc://policy.jsonc/execApprovals/agents/allowSecurity
- policy/exec-approvals-agent-security-unapproved -> oc://exec-approvals.json/defaults, requirement oc://policy.jsonc/execApprovals/agents/allowSecurity, message uses inherited defaults
- policy/exec-approvals-auto-allow-skills-enabled -> oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/main, requirement oc://policy.jsonc/execApprovals/agents/allowAutoAllowSkills
- policy/exec-approvals-auto-allow-skills-enabled -> oc://exec-approvals.json/defaults, requirement oc://policy.jsonc/execApprovals/agents/allowAutoAllowSkills

--- policy compare --json (redacted) ---
ok=true exitCode=0 rulesChecked=5 findings=[]
proof-exit=0
```

## RFC and merge gate
This PR should stay draft until maintainers accept the RFC direction for artifact-backed policy evidence and the exec-approval security surface. The intended policy scope is the RFC addition in openclaw/rfcs#6: policy may observe named product artifacts such as `exec-approvals.json` only through explicit policy rules, emit redacted evidence, and avoid runtime enforcement or credential/session stores.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/policy-state.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:extensions`
- `pnpm check:docs`
- scoped overlay docs list includes `execApprovals` for `agentIds`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/config.plugin-validation.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-registry.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- NUL-byte guard on policy TypeScript files
- real behavior proof through `policyCheckCommand` and `policyCompareCommand` in an isolated WSL temp workspace using `$OPENCLAW_HOME/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json`
- `codex review -c model="gpt-5.5" --base origin/main` clean after removing the policy-only exec approvals path setting and whitelisting allowlist entry source evidence
- Policy-overall `codex review -c model="gpt-5.5"` clean after the stacked strictness/location fixes

## Related
- RFC update: openclaw/rfcs#6

Draft while the policy evidence roadmap is being reviewed.

target.title: fix(telegram): suppress failure fallback when source delivery is message-tool-only
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #90091 reports that Telegram group turns using `message_tool_only` source delivery still emit a spurious `"No response generated. Please try again."` fallback message after the agent already delivered the visible reply via the `message` tool.
- **Root Cause**: `shouldSendFailureFallback` in `bot-message-dispatch.ts` is gated on `!isRoomEvent` but not on `!suppressSilentReplyFallback`. `suppressSilentReplyFallback` is set to `true` when `dispatchResult.sourceReplyDeliveryMode === "message_tool_only"` — but `dispatch-from-config.ts` can resolve `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` to `"message_tool_only"` via config (e.g. `visibleReplies === "message_tool"`) even for `user_request` events where `isRoomEvent = false`. When the message tool delivers the visible reply and the main reply lane is skipped with reason `"empty"` (`skippedNonSilent = 1`), the failure fallback fires.
- **Fix**: Add `!suppressSilentReplyFallback` to the `shouldSendFailureFallback` condition. The sibling silent-fallback branch at the same site already carries this guard; this aligns the failure-fallback branch with the same invariant.
- **What changed**: `bot-message-dispatch.ts` — one-line guard addition; `bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` — one regression test covering the non-room-event group turn with non-silent skip + `message_tool_only` dispatch result.
- **What did NOT change**: Config surface unchanged. Plugin surface unchanged. Gateway protocol unchanged.

### Reproduction

1. Configure a Telegram group with `visibleReplies: "message_tool"` (or equivalent global/agent-level policy)
2. Send a message that triggers a `user_request` event (e.g. mention the bot)
3. The agent delivers a reply via `message(action="send")` and returns `NO_REPLY` as the final text
4. The main reply lane is skipped with reason `"empty"` (`skippedNonSilent = 1`)
5. **Before this PR**: Telegram sends `"No response generated. Please try again."` as a delivery-mirror fallback, even though the agent already replied via message tool
6. **After this PR**: No fallback message is sent; the turn completes cleanly

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#90091)**: after a `message_tool_only` turn where the message tool delivered the visible reply, Telegram no longer emits a spurious `"No response generated. Please try again."` fallback from the failure-fallback branch

**Real environment tested (source inspection; no live Telegram replay performed)**: `dispatch-from-config.ts` confirmed to resolve `sourceReplyDeliveryMode === "message_tool_only"` independently of `isRoomEvent` when `visibleReplies === "message_tool"` via config; `bot-message-dispatch.ts` confirmed to set `suppressSilentReplyFallback = true` from that dispatch result while leaving `isRoomEvent = false`; reporter's session transcript (`2026-06-04T00:07:02.309Z` delivery-mirror messages) provided live evidence of the failure

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`; `git diff --check`; `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`

**Evidence after fix (Vitest output for the touched test file)**:

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  104 passed (104)
    Duration  25.66s
```

The new test `does not send failure fallback when source delivery is message-tool-only and reply lane is non-silently skipped` calls `dispatcherOptions.onSkip` with `reason: "empty"` (setting `skippedNonSilent = 1`) and returns `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"`, then asserts `deliverReplies` is not called. Without the fix the test fails because `shouldSendFailureFallback` evaluates to `true`.

**Observed result after fix**: `deliverReplies` not called when `suppressSilentReplyFallback = true` regardless of `isRoomEvent`; the failure-fallback branch is now aligned with the sibling silent-fallback branch which already carried this guard

**What was not tested**: live Telegram group replay with a configured `visibleReplies: "message_tool"` policy end-to-end; reporter's session transcript confirmed the source path

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: Suppressing the failure fallback for a `message_tool_only` turn where the message tool itself also failed. **Mitigation**: `suppressSilentReplyFallback` is only `true` when the dispatch result reports `message_tool_only` — meaning the dispatch layer decided the turn was handled by the message tool; the sibling silent-fallback branch carries the same guard with the same invariant.
- **Risk**: A `message_tool_only` turn where delivery genuinely failed could silently swallow the error. **Mitigation**: `dispatchError` still fires the fallback unconditionally before `suppressSilentReplyFallback` is evaluated; only the non-error skipped/failed delivery-summary path is suppressed.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Telegram

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #90091
